schools.summary: Ellicott reads 'began to reign' as simply 'reigned' and checks the synchronism; Clarke frames the whole chapter's argument.
Gill reckons the overlap of Ahaziah's and Jehoram's reigns and the twelve-year length of the new king's rule.
Henry: better than Ahab and Jezebel yet a half-reformer who keeps the calves; those repent falsely who part only with the sins they lose by.
Ellicott: 'image' is a pillar removed from Baal's temple, later restored under Jezebel who outlived Jehoram; K&D mark the seam before the war.
Gill states the charge plainly: he was guilty of idolatry.
Clarke: he abolished Baal's worship but kept the calves at Dan and Bethel.
Gill: he closely adhered to the calf-worship set up by Jeroboam.
Ellicott anchors the calf-sin in Jeroboam's apostasy and the standing formula against the northern kings.
Wesley: the calves were kept as a wall of partition between Israel and Judah; his little religion could not over-rule this policy.
Ellicott: Mesha ('salvation') is named on the Moabite Stone; noqed is a sheep-owner (cf. Amos); tribute paid in kind, a heavy burden.
Gill: Mesha's wealth consisted chiefly in flocks, from which he grew rich.
Clarke links noqed to marking/branding sheep; Wesley: his great wealth in cattle emboldened him to rebel.
K&D: the note is repeated because it occasions the campaign; Joram hastens to recover what Ahaziah neglected; Ellicott: 'rebel' = refuse the annual tribute.
Gill: Ahaziah, through weakness, sickness, or a short reign, took no steps to reduce Moab.
Ellicott: 'numbered' = mustered a levy 'in that day'; K&D: Jehoram raised an army and gained Jehoshaphat's ready promise.
Gill: the muster followed at once upon his accession.
Henry: no sooner in honour than in war; Judah and Israel unite against a common foe, yet there is nothing got by being yoked with unbelievers.
Ellicott: the pledge 'I am as thou art' repeats Jehoshaphat's word to Ahab, a mark of one hand; he assented despite prophetic censure.
Clarke: the same friendship maintained with the son as the father; Wesley defends the war as just and prudent for Jehoshaphat.
Ellicott maps the two routes: the shorter northern way risked Moab's defences and the Syrians, so the southern road through Edom was chosen.
Gill frames the question as which way was best to reach Moab, the short way over Jordan or another.
Clarke: the Edom route was taken because the tributary king of Edom would join them.
Henry: a threefold cord is not easily broken — yet the cunning route runs straight into a waterless trap.
K&D and Ellicott trace the seven-day circuit through the glen of El-Ahsy; the army lost its way in Seir and the expected water failed.
Clarke: an ill-planned march into a waterless country; Wesley explains the slow, wide circuit and identifies Edom's 'king' as Jehoshaphat's viceroy.
Henry: God lets his people's improvidence humble them, that his providence may be glorified in their relief.
Ellicott trims the Hebrew of the cry; K&D read the whole distress as designed to humble the king and work repentance.
Gill: the author of the affair throws the blame upon the Lord and his providence.
Clarke hears a guilty conscience: 'God has judicially blinded us.'
Henry: he called the kings together, yet frets against God — the foolishness of man perverts his way, then his heart frets against the Lord.
Ellicott: the same question Jehoshaphat asked in Ahab's camp; faith seeks Jehovah where Jehoram despairs; a hidden servant knew the prophet, as Obadiah did.
Gill: the good king should have inquired before setting out, yet it was not too late.
Wesley: his neglect brought the suffering, but affliction recalls him to duty; God went before them, sending Elisha unasked.
Henry: God provides his oracles for those who provide them not for themselves; he that will be great, let him learn to minister.
Ellicott: Jehoshaphat trusts Elisha on Elijah's reputation; the kings go down to the prophet as ordinary men, showing his esteem; K&D: humbled by misfortune.
Gill: Jehoshaphat inferred it from Elisha's being Elijah's disciple and successor.
Clarke: he has the gift of prophecy; Wesley: they went to honour and so engage him.
Henry: he that humbled himself was exalted, when three kings came to knock at his door.

themes: 'the word of the Lord is with him'
esteem of the man of God

schools.summary: Ellicott: 'the prophets of thy father' are Baal's prophets and false prophets of Jehovah, not yet rooted out; the plea 'three kings' leans on the number.
Gill: the prophet refuses discourse with an idolater.
Clarke calls it a just but cutting reproof; Wesley turns it to a taunt — let the idols you served now help you.
Henry: Elisha was not imposed on by a partial reformation; the reproof strips away the last false refuge.
Ellicott: 'before whom I stand' = as a minister; 'regard the presence' = lift the face; Jehoshaphat is accepted for his dependence on Jehovah; K&D: the speech still aims to humble.
Gill: the God whose minister and prophet Elisha was, ready to serve.
Clarke: Jehoshaphat worshipped the true God, Jehoram an idolater; Wesley: wicked men fare better for the society of the godly.
Henry: as a subject Elisha honours the king, as a prophet he tells even a king, 'Thou art wicked.'

themes: the wicked spared for the righteous

schools.summary: Ellicott: composure was essential to hear God; cites Cicero on the Pythagoreans; some texts read 'the Spirit of the Lord.'
Gill: the harper was called to allay his passion and fit him for prophetic inspiration.
Clarke contrasts the serene true prophet with the convulsed heathen priest; Wesley: the 'hand of the Lord' marks a gift, not a natural power.
Henry: sweetly sung praises cheer and settle the mind; a quiet spirit is fit for divine visitation.

themes: the hand of the Lord as gift

schools.summary: Ellicott: the 'valley' is a nahal/wady (Zered), the 'ditches' literally 'pits, pits' to gather run-off; the oracle bears the stamp of historic truth.
Gill: the pits were dug to receive water enough for the whole army and cattle.
Clarke pictures the dry channel enlarged into canals and reservoirs, wide enough to catch the deceiving sun.
Henry: to try their faith he bids them dig; those who expect God's blessings must prepare room for them.

themes: make the valley full of ditches

schools.summary: Ellicott: wind is the usual precursor of rain in the east; distinguishes 'that valley' from 'this,' and cattle (miqneh) from beasts of burden (behemah).
Gill: they would perceive neither the south wind nor gathering clouds nor rain.
Henry: Elijah fetched water from the clouds, but Elisha from no one knows where — God is not tied to second causes.

themes: God not tied to second causes
trusting the promise not the signs

schools.summary: Ellicott: 'a light thing' (future); the promise to deliver Moab reverses Jehoram's own despairing words about being delivered into Moab's hand.
Gill measures the two gifts: water was the lesser, victory the greater.
Henry: God gives richly, more than we can ask or think; those who seek the dew of grace are made more than conquerors.
Ellicott reads the verbs as prediction, not command, dissolving the clash with the law; K&D restrict the law to Canaanite land, not enemy Moab.
Gill canvasses several reconciliations: besieged-city trees only, or Canaanite cities, or a one-time dispensation to punish Moab.
Clarke insists fruit-trees are not meant, only trees useful for fortification; Wesley justifies the severity by Moab's implacable wickedness.

debates.question: Does the felling of Moab's trees conflict with the law of Deuteronomy 20:19?

sides.position: No — the verbs foretell what the victors would do, they do not command it
No — the law protects only Canaanite land to be occupied, not an enemy's country like Moab
The fruit-trees are not meant at all; only trees serviceable for fortification

schools.summary: Ellicott: the water came from a distant storm on Seir yet the miracle lies in the prediction and the timing; a striking coincidence with the hour of worship.
Gill ties the 'meat offering' to the daily sacrifice at Jerusalem.
Clarke calls it altogether miraculous, no natural means; Wesley: God timed it to vindicate Jerusalem's worship against the northern kingdom.
Henry: the flood did double service, relieving Israel and deceiving Moab; God honours his own instituted sacrifice.

debates.question: Was the water a pure miracle or a providentially timed natural flood?

sides.position: A natural flash-flood from a storm on Seir, the miracle lying in the prophet's prediction and the exact timing
Altogether miraculous, with no natural means, since there was neither wind nor rain

themes: water from the way of Edom

schools.summary: Ellicott unfolds the Hebrew: a levy en masse, 'from every one girding a girdle and upwards,' stationed on the frontier; K&D: all able to bear arms mobilized.
Gill names the three kings whose advance the Moabites had heard.
Henry: they promised themselves easy dealing with an army worn by the long march — the very confidence that would betray them.
Ellicott sets the optics: the sun rose behind the Moabites and reddened the water, perhaps with a pun on 'adom / Edom, the red earth.
Gill: they rose early to watch and guard against their enemies.
Clarke: an optical deception; never having seen the valley full, they took the red water for the blood of the confederates.
Henry: knowing the valley dry, they reasoned soundly from a false premise; God let them deceive themselves.

themes: the same water, life and death

schools.summary: Ellicott: the guess was 'not improbable' — allied races often fell out, and the wady was known to be dry; roused for plunder, they rushed on in disorder.
Gill: confident it was blood, having no notion of water there.
Clarke: they came on disorderly and fell an easy prey.
Henry: they believed what they wished — 'what we wish we readily believe'; those to be destroyed are first deceived.
Ellicott works the tangled Hebrew (they 'entered the country, destroying as they went'); the Moabites were unprepared for resistance.
Gill: they came not in rank and file but in a confused scramble for booty.
Henry: Israel, animated by Elisha's assurance of victory, fell on them with fury and pursued them into their own land.
K&D and Ellicott note the ruin fulfils Elisha's prophecy to the letter; only Kir-haraseth's walls held, the chief stronghold on its chalk cliff.
Gill: they demolished the walls and houses wherever they came.
Clarke and Wesley describe the siege — slingers smiting the men on the walls and slinging stones to breach them while the army sapped the foundations.
Ellicott: the garrison giving way, the king aimed his sortie at Edom as the weakest or most yielding point in the alliance.
Gill: the close siege and the slingers' execution showed the city would soon fall.
Clarke: seven hundred picked men made a desperate sortie to regain open country, but were driven back.
Henry: a bold and brave attempt against the mercenary Edomites — yet even Edom proved too hard for him.

Ellicott: 'great wrath fell upon Israel' normally denotes divine wrath, yet he takes it here that the wrath of Chemosh fell on the Hebrews, a mark of the record's antiquity; K&D: the son offered to Chemosh to appease his wrath.
Gill settles that the victim was Moab's own son and heir, not Edom's.
Clarke offers gentler readings — God's displeasure, the nations' horror, or Israel's own terror raising the siege — and contrasts the Son of man who came to save.
Henry: the indignation was for driving him to this extremity; it is well not to urge the worst of men to extremities.

debates.question: What is the 'great indignation/wrath against Israel'?

sides.position: A divine visitation — indeed the wrath of Chemosh, whom ancient Israel granted a real potency, falling on the Hebrew alliance
Not a supernatural stroke but God's displeasure at their pressing to extremity, the nations' abhorrence, or Israel's own horror, which raised the siege

themes: the mystery of the 'wrath against Israel'
